title: Insulating to the eaves in a loft
subtitle: Maintaining ventilation while filling the insulation gap at the roof edge
excerpt: The eaves are the hardest part of a loft to insulate well. The gap between the roof slope and the ceiling joist thins out at the wall plate. Without eaves baffles, either the ventilation gap is blocked (causing condensation) or the insulation stops short (leaving a cold bridge). This guide covers how to insulate correctly to the eaves without compromising either.
sourceNotes: BRE Good Repair Guide 32 on loft insulation at eaves. NHBC Standards Chapter 7.1 on roofs. PAS 2030:2023 guidance on insulating to roof eaves. Knauf mineral wool product installation guidance for cold loft applications.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rigid ventilation tray or channel fitted between the rafters at the eaves. It holds the insulation back from the eaves vent and maintains a 50 mm air gap above the insulation for ventilation. Also called a ventilation tray or rafter roll.

A point or line in the building envelope where heat flows more readily than through the surrounding insulated area. Uninsulated wall plates, joist ends, and window frames are common cold bridges. Cold bridges show up as cold patches on internal surfaces and can cause local condensation and mould.

content.text: Most loft insulation guides focus on the flat ceiling section. The eaves are harder and often done wrong. There are two failure modes: blocking the ventilation at the eaves, which causes roof condensation; and leaving a gap in the insulation at the wall plate, which creates a
at the edge of every room in the house.

A cold roof loft must be ventilated to prevent condensation forming on the rafters and roof deck. The standard requires 25 mm of continuous airflow at the eaves. If insulation is packed tight to the eaves, this path is blocked. Blocked eaves ventilation is the most common cause of roof timber decay in UK houses with loft insulation.
Before laying new insulation, fit an
between each pair of rafters at the eaves. The baffle holds the insulation away from the eaves and maintains the 50 mm ventilation channel above.
Push the insulation firmly up against the baffle so there is no gap between the insulation and the vertical face of the baffle. The insulation should cover the wall plate below.

The first 300 to 400 mm of insulation at the eaves will be thinner than the main ceiling area because the rafter space limits depth. Accept a reduced thickness here (80 to 100 mm is typical) rather than blocking the vent.
Over the main ceiling area, lay insulation to the full target depth (typically 270 mm total for a cold loft). The full depth can only start where the ceiling height gives enough rafter clearance.

On a cold morning, frost on the roof tiles melts slowly and evenly across the roof slope. The eaves area does not show early melt (which would indicate heat escaping at the wall plate). The loft has no condensation on the rafters or roof deck in winter.

The insulation is stuffed against the eaves with no baffle fitted. The eaves vent is blocked. Within three to five winters, black mould or rot appears on the rafter feet and wall plate. Alternatively, the insulation stops 300 mm short of the wall plate, leaving the wall plate fully exposed and the ceiling edge cold.

If rafter timbers or the wall plate show any signs of decay, softness, or previous damp staining, address the structural issue before adding insulation. Insulation traps moisture and will make a damp problem worse. A roofer or structural engineer should inspect any suspect timber before work starts.
